root_text.sn13.3:1.2: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, yatthimā mahānadiyo saṁsandanti samenti, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn13.3:1.3: gaṅgā yamunā aciravatī sarabhū mahī, tato puriso dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni uddhareyya.
root_text.sn13.3:1.5: katamaṁ nu kho bahutaraṁ, yāni vā dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni ubbhatāni yaṁ vā sambhejjaudakan”ti?
root_text.sn13.3:2.2: appamattakāni dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni ubbhatāni.
root_text.sn13.3:2.3: Neva satimaṁ kalaṁ upenti na sahassimaṁ kalaṁ upenti na satasahassimaṁ kalaṁ upenti sambhejjaudakaṁ upanidhāya dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni ubbhatānī”ti.

translation_text.sn13.3:1.2: “Mendicants, there are places where the great rivers—the Ganges, Yamuna, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—join together and converge.
translation_text.sn13.3:1.3: Suppose a person was to draw two or three drops of water from such a place.
translation_text.sn13.3:1.5: Which is more: the two or three drops drawn out or the water in the confluence?”
translation_text.sn13.3:2.1: “Sir, the water in the confluence is certainly more.
translation_text.sn13.3:2.2: The two or three drops drawn out are tiny.
translation_text.sn13.3:2.3: Compared to the water in the confluence, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.”
translation_text.sn13.3:2.4: “In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …”
